name: gig-worker-rights
description: Rights, protections, tax obligations, and benefit access for independent contractors, gig workers, and freelancers. For career changers doing gig work while studying.
source: Liberation Labs mutual aid knowledge base

triples.subject: ABC test
triples.object: worker classification in many states — worker is an employee UNLESS: (A) free from control, (B) performing work outside usual business, AND (C) independently established in that trade

triples.subject: misclassified employee
triples.object: wage claim with state labor agency or IRS Form SS-8 for back wages, overtime, benefits, and employer tax contributions

triples.subject: independent contractor
triples.object: quarterly estimated taxes (federal + state + self-employment) using Form 1040-ES — penalties apply for underpayment

triples.subject: self-employment tax
triples.object: 15.3% on net earnings (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare) — this is in ADDITION to income tax

triples.subject: gig worker
triples.object: business expenses including mileage (67 cents/mile in 2024), phone/internet (business percentage), supplies, home office, and health insurance premiums

triples.subject: self-employed health insurance deduction
triples.object: deducting 100% of health insurance premiums from gross income (not just itemized) — marketplace plans qualify

triples.subject: gig worker
triples.object: marketplace health insurance with income-based subsidies — self-employment income counts for subsidy calculations

triples.subject: unemployment for gig workers
triples.object: state law and classification — some states extend benefits to gig workers under ABC test rulings; pandemic-era PUA expired but set precedent

triples.subject: platform deactivation
triples.object: arbitration clause in the platform's terms of service, state labor board complaint if misclassified, or small claims court

triples.subject: gig worker
triples.object: all income (even if no 1099 received), all expenses with receipts, and mileage log — IRS requires reporting ALL income regardless of platform reporting thresholds

triples.subject: 1099-NEC threshold
triples.object: $600 — platforms must issue 1099-NEC for payments of $600+ but you must report ALL income even below this threshold

triples.subject: Qualified Business Income deduction
triples.object: deducting up to 20% of net self-employment income from taxable income — available to sole proprietors and single-member LLCs

triples.subject: gig worker retirement savings
triples.object: SEP-IRA (up to 25% of net earnings), Solo 401(k) (up to $23,000 + 25%), or traditional/Roth IRA — no employer match but tax advantages

triples.subject: workers compensation
triples.object: independent contractors — consider occupational accident insurance as a substitute, especially for delivery/driving work

triples.subject: gig worker organizing
triples.object: freelancer unions (Freelancers Union), platform-specific driver/worker groups, and advocating for portable benefits legislation

triples.subject: tax withholding
triples.object: gig workers — set aside 25-30% of every payment for taxes, or make quarterly estimated payments to avoid year-end shock

triples.subject: multi-app strategy
triples.object: income from ALL platforms is combined for tax purposes — you file one Schedule C covering all gig work, not separate filings per app

triples.subject: business license
triples.object: your city or county for gig work — check local requirements, as operating without one can result in fines

triples.subject: contract review
triples.object: read the arbitration clause, non-compete provisions, and IP assignment in platform terms — these affect your rights if disputes arise

triples.subject: gig-to-employment transition
triples.object: negotiate salary to account for lost deductions and self-employment tax savings — a $50/hour gig rate does NOT equal $50/hour salary
